item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is by Philippe Crassous and is Rights Managed. As such, it is not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: Re: * Laughs Microscopically
item.content:encoded: Ah yes. That's where I've seen this picture :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good. I probably should have done my homework but reading this was worth it.
+1

item.title: * Laughs Microscopically
item.content:encoded: Mwahahahahahahahahahahaha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i was all ready to hit this with a downvote for referencing a non-existant document (HATE that), but those interview logs…man, those interview logs. the editing you did of whatever narminess may have been in draft was done well. +1. hopefully we get to see Document SCP-1707-6-E at some point.

item.title: Potential Error
item.content:encoded:
"They, ah, they they mostly left your mouth alone."

I know this may be stuttering, but it does seem right to ask if you meant for "they" to be repeated twice in the bolded section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get one think. Why Foundation won't offer Agent Blackbox mercy kill? They have another human host, SCP-1707-06, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The swearing was a little OTT, but that last interview hit me right in the feels. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This occurs in the future, relative to the Theta-90 series. In the "present" of SCP-1707, Dimaccio transferred out of the Task Forces following injury on a deployment and now works as a Researcher, and usually acting Senior Researcher (which he isn't promoted to for real because he only has a BS, not a Master's or PhD).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would ya believe I hadn't gotten around to reading this SCP til now? Not a good idea to read this just before bed… and the addenda gave me a massive sad. Fitting that I'd read this just after having read this. So, are the events leading to SCP-1707's initial containment occurs sometime in the future, from the perspective of the MTF Theta-90 series, or in the past? The footnotes state Dimaccio was team leader for 5 years, and SCP-1707-5-b7 addendum 6 (7 years after infestation) indicates he transferred out 3 years earlier…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've been shot in the feels. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That addenda cuts deep. Very nice work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reading really freaks me out. So upvoted for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huzzah. I just thought I'd go ahead and ask, because someone eventually would, and I figured I could do it the most politely. I have to say, though, I don't really feel one way or another about Agent Blackboxes(that is his real damn name in my headcanon, the Foundation just actually-blackboxes it because they're dicks) or the researcher. They're perfectly good & realistic characters, I just find the whole "I am technically the Worm That Walks" effect to be far more interesting than the inter-personnel stuff. Though "why aren't they squished?" is one of the best lines on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have not read it, no, although it doesn't sound that much like this entry. I'm using worms, rather than mites, and the inspiration for the mechanism was the many parasitic worms that burrow through the skin to get to wherever they want to go. I pretty much thought: "What if they just stop at the dermis and survive from there?"

Also, as was pointed out to me when I pitched this for critique pre-posting, 'parasitic worm' is a very common theme, and 'long-term testing' is also pretty common - though what I was really going for in the test logs was the story of Researcher Dimaccio and Agent Blackboxes. If anything, it's more using Blackboxes to tell us about Dimaccio, who he is as a person and what choices he may be prepared to make.

Gonna have to read that story, though. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
..wait.

Are we sure that's all this is? Coincidence? Because that's nigh-exactly precisely what this SCP is. Mr Carbon, are you aware of this story, ever read it? I'm not accusing you of plagiarism, it could just be extreme coincidence(it has happened a startlingly large amount of times here), just want to make sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me eerily of the 2004 short story "Send me a Mentagram" by Dominic Green. That had ordinary skin mites suddenly and inexplicably switching from eating dead skin to eating live skin, and only skin; it also centered around the experience of several test subjects during long term captive exposure by sinister researchers…strange coincidence!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviews were literally perfect. Not many people can make a true to life sounding character in an interview great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's awesome! :D Thank you for picking what was my first ever entry for the Chinese readers to see. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear that +1 because I just find this entry interesting. It does not since I'v translated it and post it on the Chinese forum!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My one criticism is that it's not clear from the description that the infestation spreads to cover (most of, I guess) the body of the host after initial contact. I only picked that up from the interviews. Prior to that I was assuming that they covered an area corresponding to the size of the initial colony. It was a bit jarring when that misperception got corrected.

That aside…EEEEEWWWWWWWWWWWWW!! yuckyuckyuckyuckyuck. Also: "…There is no associated anesthetic effect…" Holy shit. Horrifying yet disturbingly plausible.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Combining a range indicator like "up to" with a vagueness term like "several".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ooh, right in the feels! These interviews are fantastic. The glimpse into the psychological trauma this thing has caused is excellent. It's an excellently written tragedy, and all of the characters feel very real and suited to their roles. Just very well done.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say I just don't see it.
It would be one thing if this was an official report, or if the character was a "white coat" lab-based professional. But these are interviews with what amounts to a soldier fresh from the trenches, a walking casualty under extreme duress, talking to an old "army" buddy.

I would find it utterly out of character and unrealistic if his dialogue was profanity free.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interviews could've been better, probably, but this definitely worked as an article. I actually really like how the guy has to be contained for something that is essentially harmless until he dies. He knows what's going on behind the scenes, roughly, and he probably knows he's not dangerous or curable, and there's something about that underlying despair that just makes this all the better. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why so? Uncertainty, use of "cm" rather than "centimeters" (not budging on that, btw - it's consistent with use of "m" for "meters" elsewhere), something else? I finally caved and just edited it to give the number range I had in my head, but "cm" is staying, damn it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the narrative of the interviews, showing just what you sign on for as Foundation personnel (the footnotes about the researcher's position were great), but honestly what I thought was the best part was "Why don't they get squished?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mah feels! :<

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It quickly eats someone's skin, all of it, replaces the skin, and then the host is killed if it is taken off. How is this not anamolous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
up to several cm thick

I'm gonna go with no here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think Agent blackboxes is adorable. Poor baby. Poor honey bun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The actual person is a 15-year veteran of a career path not dissimilar from being a Foundation agent, except, you know, in real life and stuff. Obviously, as above, this does not Prove You Wrong!!! because your stylistic preferences for articles are totally valid, but I just wanted to state that so that it doesn't come off like I dropped all those F-bombs in there just to be the Coolest Sweary New Kid. (Also, there's kind of a progression in Dr Xiao -> Dimaccio -> BLACKBOXES, in terms of language, from most to least formal; this is intentional.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get that there are actual people that talk like this. But I expect Foundation agents to not talk like WWE fans after seven beers, even if infected by a skip.

I don't hate this or anything, but it just kept nagging me to the point where my mouse drifted away from the upvote button because of all the swearing. To each his own, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agent Blackboxes is based on a real person, fyi. Not that that necessarily renders your comment invalid, but I had to go through and reduce the amount of cursing from 'the real guy' levels before I posted the entry. I'll suck up the downvote, though I appreciate the (amusingly delivered) crit, because I don't want to change his character any further away from the Agent Blackboxes in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, I really wanna upvote this fuckin' skip, but I got a few too many goddamn problems with it. You got this bullshit description with this weak ass creature, so you gotta rely on this fuckin' series of addendums. Now, don't get me wrong here, ain't no fuckin' problem with that. Hell, SCP-342 did that and it's one of the best goddamn things on this site. But Agent shithead, sorry, blackbox, has a bit of a fuckin' foul mouth on him. Got to the point where I wanted him to shut the fuck up because he was swearing so goddamn much.

So sorry man, but I gotta give this shit a fuckin' negative one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, you should have seen my initial draft of that segment. It was narm to the maxxxxx. I re-read it, Cringe Mode: On, and slice-and-diced it to its present form. Conveying the sads without narming all over the place is a fine line sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the last interview almost crossed into narm territory, but the rest of it, while a tad predictable, was pretty well written. Reminded me of Azzleflux's dialogue, in a good way. I also like the whole imagery of the worm-skin stuff. You have earned an upvote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm just tired, but I'm not seeing what's anomalous about this. Upvoted anyways because it still freaked me out; I just don't know what the Foundation has to do with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I left that ambiguity in deliberately, because I liked both main ways it could be interpreted: either Ethics Committee Members Level 3 And Above Only, or Level 3 And Up By Authorization Of Ethics Committee. The involvement of the Ethics Committee, to a greater or lesser extent, is a theme in several of the ideas I have at the moment. I think they're one of the most underused parts of the fictional Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked it. It was a very good read and a good story…great pic by the way. The only thing I've got to say is this phrase:

several cm

You might consider either giving a rough number to that or spelling out centimeters. It works well enough even without the change, but that struck me as odd. All around, though, definitely upvote worthy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially a little iffy about this, because it seemed like the Foundation was being unnecessarily cruel to poor Agent █████. But looking at it again, that's not really the case. They gave him a proper bed after it became obvious he might not get out for some time, if ever, and the initial bad vibes I was getting off Dimaccio's footnotes (and subsequent site transfer) went away once I read them in the context of a former MTF superior watching his friend and teammate sink into depression and anger without being able to do anything about it. Realistically, Agent █████ is probably being treated quite well for an SCP victim-turned-object. Whatever privileges he's given are just not mentioned in the article proper, since he's a special case and therefore they're not necessary for containment.

Anyway, even before I came to that realization, I was still going to upvote. Whether I agreed with their treatment of Agent █████ or not, you told the story you wanted to tell and I enjoyed it.

One thing confuses me a little, though. This collapsible:

Ethics Committee. Level 3 and above only.

I can't quite figure out what the Ethics Committee part is referring to. Is it saying that in order to access the interviews, you must both have Level 3 clearance and be a member of the Ethics Committee? The wording is a bit unclear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Interview was kinda long, but it kept me interested. Life as a disgusting, suicidal skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP isn't bad on its own, but I think the addenda really sell it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm reluctant to remove large amounts of the dialog, though I just went through and removed a couple of bits that, on umpteenth inspection, were probably redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Same person. One is his full name. The other is his last name. I wrote the full things out and blackboxed after the fact.

E: Removed the blackbox from the space in the dude's full name to make this more clear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it was so out-of-the-blue that I thought the SCP was affecting his mind.

It is, indirectly. Just not with any woo-woo stuff or anomalous properties. I edited the dialog in that section slightly to hopefully make that a bit more clear, via Dr Xiao's reaction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how you have now established a narrative with this, but like Silber said, it's been done before. The anomalous creature(s) here still don't feel anomalous enough for me; just a few of these things on a person won't hurt them. Also, maybe if there was a little less dialogue I would have been impacted more at the end.

Sorry, but I'm going to have to downvote for now.

Edit: Looking back on this, I know that this has been done before, but the way it was executed wasn't bad at all. Cancelling my vote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like this. The actual SCP is pretty much a framing device for a story told in the addenda, and that's something I've used before to strong effect.

The dialogue could stand to be a little stronger, though. That "Jenny from the block" comment sailed over my head; yeah, I get the reference, but it was so out-of-the-blue that I thought the SCP was affecting his mind.

At its core, this is another commentary of the Foundation as one of the least ethical prison systems on its planet, and while these are going to pop up until the site's perception of his humanoid containment works… I don't know. I would hope the Foundation could find a sane way around that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1707-5: One (1) human, formerly Agent ██████████, infested by SCP-1707 during initial discovery and containment

Agent █████: Skip-1707-5.

Are these supposed to be different people? Or just blackbox inconsistency?

item.title: SCP-1707, 'New Skin'
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everyone who offered critique - in particular, those who recommended I go back to the original concept of the interview-driven article. (If you don't like interviews, or collapsibles - sorry!)

I think I caught all the typos and such, but if you find any feel free to make the usual minor edits for good English.

E: Forgot to mention: the image is a recolored electron micrograph of a real worm (a deep-ocean polychaete).
